intro: Welcome to your Robotics & AI adventure! 🤖 Work through the lessons in order. Read the notes, look at the pictures, try the activities — then take the lesson quiz to earn XP. You learn best by doing, so have fun and don't be afraid to experiment!

lessons.title: Lesson 1 — Robotics & AI Basics
lessons.summary: What is a robot? What is AI? Where do we see them in real life?

notes.text: A robot is a machine that can sense the world around it, think about what to do, and then act — like moving, beeping, or lighting up. Robots do jobs that are boring, dangerous, or very precise.

Every robot has three main parts:
👀 Sensors — to feel the world (like eyes and ears).
🧠 A controller (brain) — to make decisions.
🦿 Actuators — motors and lights that make it move or react.

AI (Artificial Intelligence) is when a computer learns to do clever things — like recognising a face, understanding speech, or spotting a cat in a photo. Just like you get better with practice, AI gets better by looking at lots of examples.

🌍 Real-world robots & AI: factory arms building cars, robot vacuum cleaners, self-driving cars, drones, voice assistants like Siri, and robots that explore Mars! In the future, robots will help even more in hospitals, farms, and homes.
🔎 Try it! Look around your home. Can you spot 3 machines that 'sense' something and react? (Hint: a fridge light, an automatic door, a phone that unlocks with your face.)

lessons.title: Lesson 2 — Arduino & Electronics
lessons.summary: Meet the Arduino board and the basic electronic parts.

notes.text: Arduino is a tiny computer board that acts as a robot's brain. You give it instructions (a program), and it controls lights, motors, and reads sensors.

🧰 Basic electronic parts you'll use:
• Resistor — slows down electricity to protect parts.
• LED — a small light 💡.
• Buzzer — makes sound 🔊.
• Capacitor — stores a little electricity.
• Diode — lets electricity flow one way only.
• DC motor — spins to make things move.
• Switch — turns a circuit on or off.
Electricity needs a complete loop called a circuit. If the loop is broken (like a switch turned off), nothing works — just like water can't flow through a cut pipe.
A breadboard lets you build circuits by pushing parts into holes — no glue or soldering needed. Perfect for trying ideas and fixing mistakes quickly!

💡 Try it! On a breadboard, light one LED using a battery and a resistor. Then add a switch so you can turn it on and off. What happens if you remove the resistor? (Ask your teacher — never skip it, it protects the LED!)

lessons.title: Lesson 3 — micro:bit Coding
lessons.summary: Program the micro:bit with easy drag-and-drop blocks.

notes.text: The micro:bit is a pocket-sized computer with 25 tiny LEDs, 2 buttons (A and B), and built-in sensors. It's a super friendly way to start coding.
You program it with blocks — colourful puzzle pieces you drag together. No typing needed at first! Later you can switch to text code when you're ready.

💡 Inputs are things the micro:bit senses: button presses, shaking, light, and temperature.
🔆 Outputs are things it does: light up LEDs, show pictures, or make sounds.
A simple idea: WHEN button A is pressed → SHOW a heart ❤️. That's input → action, the same 'sense and act' idea from Lesson 1!
🎮 Try it! In the micro:bit editor, make a program: when you press button A, show a smiley 😀; when you press button B, show a heart ❤️. Then try 'on shake → show a random number' to build a dice!

lessons.title: Lesson 4 — Arduino Programming
lessons.summary: Write code for the Arduino using the Arduino IDE and Tinkercad.

notes.text: We write Arduino programs in the Arduino IDE (a free app) and can test them for free online in Tinkercad before using real parts.

Every Arduino program has two main parts:
• setup() — runs once at the start (get things ready).
• loop() — runs again and again forever (the robot's heartbeat).

🧱 Building blocks of code:
• Variables store information (like a labelled box).
• Loops repeat actions.
• Conditionals (if / else) make decisions: IF it's dark → turn the light ON.

Arduino pins can be INPUT (read a sensor) or OUTPUT (control an LED or motor). The Serial Monitor lets the Arduino print messages to your screen — great for checking what's happening and fixing bugs 🐞.
✨ Try it! In Tinkercad, make an LED blink: turn it ON, wait 1 second, turn it OFF, wait 1 second — forever. Change the waiting time to make it blink faster or slower!

lessons.title: Lesson 5 — Sensors & Actuators
lessons.summary: How robots feel the world and how they move.

notes.text:
Sensors are the robot's senses (inputs). Some common ones:
• Ultrasonic — measures distance with sound 🔊.
• IR — spots objects or follows a line.
• LDR — senses light (bright or dark).
• Temperature — senses hot or cold.
• Touch / limit — senses a press or bump.

Actuators make the robot do things (outputs):
• DC motor — spins wheels to drive around.
• Servo motor — turns to an exact angle (great for arms and steering).
• LED & buzzer — light and sound to show what's happening.
The magic is putting them together: a sensor gives the Arduino information, the Arduino decides, and an actuator acts. This is called feedback control.
🎯 Try it! Idea for a mini-project: use an ultrasonic sensor so a buzzer beeps faster as your hand gets closer — like a car parking sensor! Or use an LDR to turn on an LED when it gets dark.

lessons.title: Lesson 6 — AI & Machine Learning
lessons.summary: Teach a computer to recognise pictures, sounds, and poses.

notes.text: Machine Learning (ML) is a type of AI where a computer learns from examples instead of being told every rule. Show it lots of cat photos and dog photos, and it learns to tell them apart!

The steps are always the same:
1️⃣ Collect examples (photos, sounds, or poses).
2️⃣ Train the model on those examples.
3️⃣ Predict — show it something new and it guesses the answer.
Google Teachable Machine is a free website 🌐 that lets you train your own AI in minutes — right in your browser. You can teach it to recognise images, sounds, or body poses.
⚖️ Important: AI is only as good as its examples. If you give it messy or unfair examples, it makes mistakes. Good examples = smart AI!
📷 Try it! On Teachable Machine, train an image model with two classes: 'thumbs up 👍' and 'thumbs down 👎'. Give lots of examples of each, then test it live with your webcam. How accurate is it?

lessons.title: Lesson 7 — Build a Robot
lessons.summary: Design, build, and program an obstacle-avoidance robot.

notes.text: Now we bring everything together to build an obstacle-avoidance robot — a robot car that drives around and turns away when it sees something in the way.

🛠️ The build steps:
1️⃣ Build the body (chassis) and attach the wheels and motors.
2️⃣ Add the Arduino brain and the battery.
3️⃣ Fit an ultrasonic sensor at the front (the robot's eyes 👀).
4️⃣ Upload the code.
5️⃣ Test and improve!

🧠 The robot's thinking (the code idea):
IF something is close in front → STOP and turn.
ELSE → drive forward.
This repeats over and over in the loop(), just like Lesson 4.
🧪 Testing & refining means trying it, seeing what goes wrong, and fixing it — maybe the turn is too small, or it stops too late. Real engineers improve their robots this way!
📓 Try it! Keep a project logbook. Draw your robot, list its parts, and write down every problem you fixed. What distance should trigger a turn — 10 cm or 20 cm? Test both and pick the best!

lessons.title: Lesson 8 — Project & Future
lessons.summary: Show off your robot and dream about what's next!

notes.text: 🎉 Project Showcase! This is where you show your robot to others, explain how it works, and maybe race it in a friendly competition. Be proud — you built a real robot!
📝 Your project report tells the story of your robot: your design idea, how you coded it, the problems you faced, and what you learned from start to finish.

🔮 The future of robotics: robots that help doctors, farm our food, clean the oceans, and explore space. AI is getting smarter every year. The skills you learned here — sensing, thinking, acting, and coding — are the same ones real engineers use!
🌟 Try it! Imagine a robot that solves a problem in YOUR life. What does it sense? What does it do? Draw it and give it a name. Who knows — you might build it one day!
